 In addition, of course, all capabilities offered by your coding agent of choice are supported and driven by our ADT MCP Server.

With the introduction of the ADT MCP Server, developers can unlock a truly agentic ABAP development experience. The ADT MCP Server makes ABAP development tools easily accessible to AI systems, streamlining development tasks, simplifying workflows, and improving efficiency.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for eclipse, see:   Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

Furthermore, step by step, we will achieve a similar level of ABAP AI capabilities support in ADT for Visual Studio Code.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for Visual Studio Code, see: Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

 

With that, go and explore the re-invented ABAP AI offering!

 Coauthors,

 @karl_kessler @niloofar_naseri

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment – Pre-Upgrade Option for Release 2608
entry.content:
Overview

Providing a significant number of new features is an important goal of release upgrades to deliver innovations to our customers and partners. There are four major release upgrades per year following a predefined schedule as described in this blog post.

An upgrade is intended as a “no event” for customers and partners. For example, only compatible changes of released objects (APIs) will be delivered. We recommend performing a test of the custom-built productive apps in a test environment for the new release. The complexity of the technical dependencies between the custom-built apps and the underlying environment cannot be tested isolated, only including the ABAP environment on SAP-side. Therefore, we offer you to pre-upgrade selected systems 29 days in advance to test the applications prior to the planned standard-upgrade. Each ABAP environment release is connected to a fixed UI5 major version. As a consequence, the pre-upgrade tests include the upcoming UI5 version as well. Please refer to this blog post for further details.   

 

Purpose

This option enables a customer or partner to test the applications they have already developed on top of the new release a couple of days before the standard-upgrade. With SAP BTP ABAP Environment (aka "Steampunk"), we follow the approach of keeping released objects stable and provide all enhancements in a compatible fashion. Early testing is an additional measure to ensure proper software quality and might be used to detect unforeseen regressions. However, this option is not intended to test new features or to consume new features earlier.

 

Approach

The standard-upgrade of all systems to release 2608 is planned for August 15/16.

A customer or partner can nominate one or more already existing systems for the pre-upgrade. A nominated system will be upgraded on July 17 within a planned downtime from 8:00 to 16:00 CET. Depending on the system settings, the actual technical downtime might be only ~20 min within the mentioned timeframe. Please refer to this blog post about the downtime-optimized upgrade. 

We recommend nominating a non-development system, such as a test system. The nomination of a development system is technically also possible, but fixes or other changes must not be imported into another system with a lower release. The nomination of a productive system doesn’t make sense because the purpose of the pre-upgrade is the re-test of the existing application before the productive system gets the new release.

According to the approach explained above, this offer is only relevant for customers or partners having at least two systems.

There might be situations where a correction of custom objects needs to be deployed. Such corrections are possible if the development / correction system is on the older release and is imported into the pre-upgraded system with the higher release.

Except potential fixes, the pre-upgrade is based on the same version as the standard-upgrade. It is planned to perform the pre-upgrade based on hotfix collection 2. The pre-upgrade systems will get the import of hotfix collection 3 after 2 weeks in the middle of the entire pre-upgrade test phase. It is planned to perform the standard-upgrade based on hotfix collection 3 plus potentially additonal patches and import these potentially additional fixes to the pre-upgrade systems prio to the upgrade weekend on August 14.

 

Costs

This offering is optional and free of any additional charges.

 

Planned New Features

The planned new features will be published on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP Environment on July 10. This list gives you an overview of potential areas for re-testing. The purpose of the pre-upgrade is not to consume these features before the standard-upgrade, because the documentation on help.sap.com will be delivered later before the standard-upgrade. It would also not be possible to transport code of such an adoption, because subsequent systems are not yet on the new release during the pre-upgrade phase.

Independent of the upgrades of the SAP BTP ABAP Environment, we recommend developing automated tests for the custom-built apps to detect regressions early on and without manual effort for re-testing. Such automated tests are perfectly suited to be run in the pre-upgrade test phase.

 

Time Schedule

The following figure summarizes the general approach:

Milestones for release 2608:

  • June 16: Nomination of system(s) is possible in the Landscape Portal
  • July 10: Announcement of the planned new features
  • July 13: Pre-upgrade systems are known
  • July 17: Pre-upgrade (with hotfix collection 2)
  • July 31: Import of hotfix collection 3 into pre-upgrade systems
  • August 12: Potential decision to postpone the upgrade
  • August 14: Import of additional patches for collection 3 into pre-upgrade systems
  • August 15/16: Standard-upgrade (with hotfix collection 3 and additonal patches)

 

System Nomination

To nominate a system, please register the system(s) by July 13 (EOB) latest by using the Landscape Portal. You can find further information about Accessing the Landscape Portal and about Manage SAP System Upgrade. Please do not create customer tickets for system nomination like in previous pre-upgrades.

With such a nomination, the customer agrees to an extraordinary planned maintenance on July 17 for the pre-upgrade and on July 31 and August 14 for the import of hotfix collections resp. additional patches (see above). You can find information about the required system downtimes in this blog post.

 

Report an Issue

Issues should be reported as part of the regression test phase by July 31 (EOB) to allow for a sufficient time frame for issue analysis and potential corrections on SAP-side.

Please create a customer ticket by using component BC-CP-ABA.

If a detected issue is likely to endanger the productive use after the standard-upgrade, you should use ticket priority very high despite the fact that the pre-upgrade system is not a productive system.

 

Exceptional Process in Case of Unresolved Priority Very High Issues

In case of the aligned decision on Wednesday, August 12 (based on unresolved priority very high tickets) to postpone the upgrade, a dedicated mitigation process needs to be started together with the respective customer or partner, with the following boundary conditions:

  • Resolving the open priority very high issues remains the highest priority for both, SAP and the customer or partner, as it is also the prerequisite for the upgrade.
  • According to the estimated issue resolution, catching up the upgrade needs to be planned.
  • The upgrade leads to a business downtime. Please refer to this blog post.
  • The upgrade must be scheduled within 7 calendar days the latest after fixing all remaining open priority very high issues.
  • We will not provide any fixes for the old release after the original date of the standard-upgrade to the new release.

In case business-critical issues are detected after the upgrade:

  • Issues need to be reported as priority very high issues and will be processed accordingly.
  • Going back to the release before upgrade is not an option. Therefore, resolving the issues in the new release with the appropriate priority is the required approach.

 

Summary

We recommend performing regression tests in pre-upgraded test systems to avoid day-1 impacts of productive custom apps after the standard-upgrade.

entry.title: Unmanaged RAP application for Excel File Upload and CDS Custom Entity with Unmanaged Query Implement
entry.content:
Intro

Nowadays, finding information is easier than ever. Although, the experience still remains the key differentiator. And that’s what I would like to share through this blog – my experience in implementing the Excel File Upload in a RAP application in BTP ABAP environment. RAP application was implemented with an unmanaged behavior, supporting also the editing of multiple items at the same time.  Additionally, the data model is based on custom entities with unmanaged queries.

 

Use-case

RAP application for maintaining Bonus Plans; allow the editing of multiple records at the same time; allow mass upload from an Excel file.

 

Data Model

The data model for my scenario is based on 2 DB tables (ZIP_BONUS_PLAN and ZIP_BP_UPLOAD) and 3 CDS Custom Entities (ZCE_BP_SINGLETON, ZCE_BONUS_PLAN and ZCE_BP_UPLOAD). Refer to Custom Entities | SAP Help Portal.

The root entity is a singleton entity aiming to implement multi-inline-edit. Refer to Developing Transactional Apps with Multi-Inline-Edit Capabilities | SAP Help Portal.

All 3 Custom Entities implement the retrieval logic in the same ABAP class ZCL_BP_UNMNG_QUERY.

In the attachment you can find the files containing the most interesting parts of coding:

  • 2 Database Tables
  • 3 CDS views
  • Unmanage Query implementation
  • Processing logic
  • RAP Behavior Definition
  • RAP Behavior Implementation

 

Implementation of Excel Upload

The implementation of File Upload is based on large objects (LOBs), providing end users the option to incorporate external files when editing entity instances. To make it work with Excel files you have to implement XCO_CP_XLSX. Refer to Working with Large Objects | SAP Help Portal and XLSX | SAP Help Portal.

 

Service Binding

For Service Binding I’ve chosen “OData V2 – UI” because if I go for “OData V4 – UI” some parts are missing and I get the following notification in the Service Binding itself “oData V4 services with no draft capability will be primarily READ-ONLY”.

 

Fiori UI Preview

The preview starts with the root entity ZCE_BP_SINGLETON for which the if_rap_query_provider builds a dummy record during runtime:

 

Here I have implemented a custom action “Drop all Data” to be able to clear my demo tables.

When I select the singleton record, the application navigates to the page with Bonus Plan Grid:

 

Here we start with the “Mass Upload” functionality, by executing the “Create New Upload Template”. As a result, a new record is created in the table ZIP_BP_UPLOAD, containing a template file attachment.

 

When you enter into the details of this record, you can download the template:

 

Maintain the Excel file data and upload it back:

 

Now, the file is uploaded and the record is updated:

 

Once the file is uploaded, it can be processed:

 

Successfully processed file will create records in the table ZIP_BONUS_PLAN. The UI allows editing multiple records:

 

Final words

In an era where AI helps us find answers faster than ever, the craft still lies in how we turn those answers into working solutions. This Excel upload journey—unmanaged behavior for bulk editing, custom entities with unmanaged queries—was my way of shaping RAP to the problem, not the other way around. If my experience saves you a few hours, a few pitfalls, or inspires a cleaner design, then sharing it was worth it. The rest is practice—and I’m looking forward to hearing about your experience in the comments below.

entry.title: Building an Inventory System with RAP on BTP Trial — CDS Views Explained
entry.content:
Introduction

When people talk about RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model), the conversation usually jumps straight to behavior definitions, actions, and determinations. The CDS layer gets a brief mention and everyone moves on.

That's a mistake.

The CDS view architecture is the contract your entire application is built on. Get it right and everything above it — behaviors, OData services, Fiori UIs — falls into place naturally. Get it wrong and you'll spend hours debugging problems that were actually design issues.

In this post I'll walk through the complete CDS layer of a Mini Inventory & Stock Movement System I built on SAP BTP Trial, covering four entities across three business objects. I'll explain the design decisions, not just the code.

The System at a Glance

The application manages warehouse stock movements. Three business objects cover the full domain:

Business Object Interface View Database Table
Material MasterZI_MiniMM_MaterialZRMZ_MM_MATERIAL
Storage LocationZI_MiniMM_StorageZRMZ_MM_STORAGE
Stock Document (Header)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentZRMZ_MM_DOC_HDR
Stock Document (Item)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItemZRMZ_MM_DOC_ITEM
Current Stock (read-only)ZI_MiniMM_CurrentStockZRMZ_MM_STOCK

Each entity has a matching projection view (ZC_ prefix) used to expose the OData service.

The Two-Layer Architecture

RAP enforces a clean separation between two CDS view types. Understanding why this separation exists is more important than understanding the syntax.

Interface views (ZI_) are the authoritative source of truth. They sit directly on the database tables and carry everything the framework needs to understand your data: semantic annotations, associations, compositions, and quantity/currency references. These views should be stable — changing them has downstream impact.

Projection views (ZC_) consume the interface views and add presentation concerns: the provider contract, @Metadata.allowExtensions for metadata extensions, and the redirection of compositions so that OData navigation points to the correct projection layer. Projection views can evolve more freely.

A useful mental model: ZI_ is the API contract, ZC_ is the UI adapter.

Composition: Stock Document Header and Item

The most important structural relationship in this model is the header-item composition between ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument and ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem.

In the header (root entity):

 
 
abap
composition [0..*] of ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem    as _Items

In the item (child entity):

 
 
abap
association to parent ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument  as _Header  on $projection.DocumentID = _Header.DocumentID

The [0..*] cardinality means a document can have zero or more items — appropriate here since a newly created document starts empty. The parent association on the item side creates the back-navigation link.

Why does this matter? Composition in RAP is not just a modelling convenience. It has runtime implications: the managed runtime treats the composition tree as a single transactional unit. When you delete a header, items are automatically deleted. When you lock a header for editing, the entire tree is locked. This is the correct behaviour for a stock document.

Associations: Two Aliases, One Target View

The item view introduces an interesting challenge: a stock movement can have both a source storage location and a target storage location, and both must be validated against the same ZI_MiniMM_Storage entity.

The solution is two separate associations pointing to the same underlying view:

 
 
abap
association [1..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Material as _Material  on $projection.MaterialID = _Material.MaterialID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _SourceStorage  on $projection.SourceStorage = _SourceStorage.StorageID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _TargetStorage  on $projection.TargetStorage = _TargetStorage.StorageID

A few design points worth noting here.

The _Material association is [1..1] — every item line must reference a valid material. _SourceStorage and _TargetStorage are both [0..1] because not every movement type uses both. A goods receipt (GR) has no source; a goods issue (GI) has no target. Making both optional at the CDS level correctly reflects the business reality.

The alias names (_SourceStorage, _TargetStorage) are different even though both navigate to ZI_MiniMM_Storage. This is required by the framework — CDS does not allow two associations with identical names on the same view, even if the target differs.

Semantic Annotations: Free Administrative Data

RAP's managed runtime will auto-populate administrative fields if you annotate them correctly. No custom code required.

 
 
abap
@Semantics.user.createdBy: truecreated_by      as CreatedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: truecreated_at      as CreatedAt,

@Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: truelast_changed_by as ChangedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: truelast_changed_at as ChangedAt

These annotations go on the interface view, not the projection. The framework reads them at runtime and fills the mapped database columns on every create and update operation.

The only requirement is that the underlying database table columns have compatible data types (SYUNAME, TIMESTAMPL respectively) and that you declare with_draft or standard managed behaviour in the behavior definition. If those conditions are met, this is genuinely zero-effort audit trail data.

Projection Views: What Most Tutorials Skip

Every interface view has a corresponding projection view. The projection view adds three things that the interface view cannot provide: the provider contract transactional_query declaration (required for OData V4 exposure), @Metadata.allowExtensions: true (required for metadata extensions to attach), and — crucially — the composition redirect.

The composition redirect is the step that most beginner tutorials either skip or explain poorly.

In the projection header:

 
 
abap
_Items : redirected to composition child ZC_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem

In the projection item:

 
 
abap
_Header : redirected to parent ZC_MiniMM_StockDocument

Without this redirect, OData navigation from the header to its items would traverse back to the interface entities (ZI_), not the projection entities (ZC_). The service binding would still activate, but navigation in Fiori would either fail silently or return data without the metadata annotations your UI depends on.

Value Help Annotations

The final piece of the CDS layer is the value help definitions, which live in the metadata extension of the item projection view.

 
 
abap
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Material',
  entity.element: 'MaterialID' }]MaterialID;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]SourceStorage;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]TargetStorage;

The annotation always points to the projection view (ZC_), not the interface view. This is because the value help dropdown in Fiori is itself an OData call, and OData services are generated from projection views. Pointing at ZI_ would fail at activation or produce no results.

Both SourceStorage and TargetStorage reference the same ZC_MiniMM_Storage projection — same entity, same element, different field. The framework handles this correctly.

Running This on BTP Trial

Everything described in this post runs on a free SAP BTP Trial account with the ABAP environment service instance. No S/4HANA system required. No license. The ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse is the only tool you need locally.

If you've been waiting for the right time to start learning ABAP Cloud and RAP, a BTP trial account is genuinely the right place to start. The constraints of the cloud environment (no classic ABAP, no access to SAP standard tables directly) force you to learn the correct patterns from the beginning.

ArchitectureMaterial View InterfaceMaterial Create InterfaceStock Movement Interface

entry.title: The Missing Layer in Every SAP Go-Live: Why Hypercare Should Be Built Before Go-Live, Not After!
entry.content:
The fifth time I watched a warehouse operative stand frozen in front of a red error screen at 6 AM on go-live day — waiting for a support consultant who wasn't there — I stopped thinking about the symptom and started thinking about the cause.

I have lived this exact scene across five SAP EWM implementations — frozen food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, tires. Different industries, different warehouses, different error messages. The same scene every time. A worker who was trained three weeks ago, on a process that has slightly changed since, hits an error the training never covered. The shift supervisor doesn't know the answer. The consultant who configured that exact process rotated off the project last week, or is asleep in a different time zone, or is on another call. The truck is waiting at the dock. And the person who is supposed to keep goods moving is standing still, holding an RF gun, looking at a screen they don't understand.

This is hypercare. And after living it five times, I've concluded we've been solving it the wrong way.

Why Hypercare Keeps Failing — Structurally

The standard answer to a rough go-live is "throw more consultants at it." More floor support. Longer hypercare window. Round-the-clock coverage. I no longer believe that's the answer, because the failures aren't random — they're structural. Six causes show up on every project:

Consultant rotation. The person who configured your wave logic, your POSC/LOSC routing, your putaway strategy, your PPF output — they are not on the floor at 6 AM on day one. The knowledge that would resolve the error in thirty seconds has already walked out the door.

The 24/7 coverage gap. Warehouses run three shifts. Hypercare staffing rarely does. The night shift hits the same errors as the day shift, with even less support.

Accent and language barriers. A global implementation means the support consultant and the floor worker often don't share a first language. Under time pressure, on a noisy dock, that gap becomes a wall.

Knowledge walkout. When hypercare ends, the consultants leave — and every hard-won resolution they discovered leaves with them. The next project rediscovers the same errors from scratch.

The same errors, every project. Across five implementations I have watched teams independently rediscover the identical handful of go-live errors — a missing PPF condition, a queue stuck in /SCWM/MON, an exception code nobody assigned. We never built an institutional memory.

The screenshot barrier. When a worker can't describe an error in words, they photograph it. That photo gets forwarded to a consultant, who interprets it, who replies. The whole support loop already runs on screenshots — we just haven't built anything to receive them.

More Consultants Is Not the Answer

Look at those six causes together and a pattern emerges: this is not a headcount problem. It is a knowledge continuity and availability problem.

Adding more junior, rotating consultants doesn't fix continuity — it amplifies the churn. What's actually missing is something that holds the project-specific knowledge, is available on every shift, speaks the worker's language, accepts a screenshot, and never rotates off.

That isn't a person. It's a system — and it should be the floor's first-level support. Not a replacement for the consultant, but the layer that sits in front of them: it answers the 70-80% of go-live questions that are known, repetitive, and project-specific, and escalates only the genuinely new problems to a human. The consultant's scarce time stops being spent re-answering the same wave error twenty times a shift, and starts being spent on the issues that actually need an architect.

Here's the part that I think is genuinely new.

An Embedded AI Agent — Built in Parallel With Testing

The concept is an AI hypercare agent embedded in the implementation. Plenty of people have proposed an "AI chatbot for SAP support," so let me be precise about the design principles that make this different — because the difference is the whole point.

1. Built in parallel with testing — not after UAT. This is the headline. Every AI support tool I have seen is deployed after go-live, then slowly trained on real incidents while real people suffer. This agent is trained during the testing phase — every SIT defect, every UAT resolution, every project-specific configuration quirk feeds it as the project happens. It arrives at go-live already 90% ready, not as a blank slate that learns while the warehouse bleeds.

2. Solution-Architect-curated, weekly. A human quality gate sits in the knowledge pipeline. Each week during testing, the SA reviews what the agent has ingested and validates it. This is what stops the noise problem that makes generic chatbots useless — the agent learns verified resolutions, not every wrong guess from a test cycle.

3. Multimodal — screenshot first. The primary interface is the photograph of the error screen, because that is already how the floor communicates. The worker shows the agent what they see, exactly as they would show a consultant.

4. A non-negotiable contracted deliverable. This is a commercial reframing, not just a technical one — and I'll come back to it, because it's the part that makes the whole thing actually happen.

5. Self-learning with a validation gate. Resolved tickets feed back into the knowledge base — but only after a Solution Architect signs off, never automatically. This principle matters enough that I give it its own section below, because it is what separates this from a chatbot that confidently repeats wrong answers.

6. Built for the human factors. Accent-independent, anxiety-aware, available to a worker who was rotated onto this line yesterday. These aren't technical features. They're the reason the gap exists, and only someone who has watched it five times would design for them.

7. A controlled, always-current knowledge surface. Beyond resolving errors, the bot is trained on the project's own documentation library — OPLs, work instructions, process guides — and returns only the latest approved version of any document. It becomes the single place the floor can ask, in plain English, "what do I do when X happens?" and trust the answer is current.

The Bot Is a System Object — So It Gets Signed Off Like One

Here's a principle I'd argue is non-negotiable: on go-live day, this bot cannot be a surprise.

In a new SAP S/4HANA embedded EWM deployment, no warehouse process reaches production without passing through the full validation chain — unit test, SIT, UAT, regression, cutover sign-off — approved by the relevant process owners. A wave release strategy is signed off by the outbound lead. A production staging flow is signed off by the manufacturing and warehouse leads. An inbound GR process is signed off by the receiving lead.

The hypercare bot must be treated as exactly that kind of object. It moves through the same gates as every warehouse process:

  • SIT — the bot's resolutions are validated against the same test scripts the processes are validated against. If a resolution it gives would fail the SIT script, it doesn't ship.
  • UAT — the actual business users — the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, the receiving specialist — interact with the bot during UAT, not for the first time on go-live morning. They sign off that its answers match how they will actually work.
  • Regression and cutover — the bot's knowledge base is version-controlled and frozen at cutover alongside the configuration, with the same change-control discipline.

And critically: the bot is trained and socialized by the Organizational Change Management (OCM) team, not dropped in by IT. The same OCM workstream that runs the training, builds the work instructions, and manages the floor's readiness is the workstream that introduces the bot. So when the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, or the inbound receiving specialist opens it at 5 AM on day one, it is a familiar tool they were trained on and helped shape during UAT — not a random, fancy, surprise bot that nobody trusts. Trust is the entire game in hypercare. A tool the floor met for the first time on go-live day has zero trust. A tool they helped validate for six weeks has earned it.

What It Actually Looks Like — Four Real Scenarios

Let me make it concrete across the warehouse flows, in an embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM deployment. These are representative of the exact class of issues that consume hypercare on every project — and each one shows a different capability the bot needs.

Outbound — the shipping coordinator and the "stock is there, but it isn't" problem. The coordinator runs the wave simulator in the shipping cockpit and it fails — the wave won't simulate. They check the warehouse monitor and the stock shows as F2, unrestricted-use in the warehouse, so by every signal on their screen the stock is available. The real cause is upstream: the batch is restricted because the QM team never released it, and the standard simulator doesn't surface that as the reason it failed. This is a genuine product gap — the simulator tells you it failed, not that a QM batch status is the cause. The bot closes that gap. The coordinator describes the failure in plain English; the bot, trained on this project's QM-EWM batch-release interplay, immediately points to the batch's QM status as the thing to check rather than the warehouse stock type — turning a forty-minute "but the stock is right there" investigation into a thirty-second answer. The same finding is also logged as a candidate enhancement to the simulator itself.

Outbound — the one-off scenario and the right version of the right document. A truck comes back to the yard because it was overloaded — a rare exception the coordinator handles maybe twice a year and has no memorized procedure for. There is an OPL for it, sitting in a shared drive among hundreds of other documents, and quite possibly in three slightly different versions. The bot was trained to reference that document library and tested to return only the latest approved version of any requested procedure. The coordinator simply asks, in plain English, what to do when a loaded truck returns — and gets the current OPL, not a superseded one, without hunting through folders or pinging a consultant to find out which version is live. This is the bot as a controlled, always-current knowledge surface over documentation that already exists but nobody can find under pressure.

Inbound — the receiving specialist who packed it wrong and needs to start over. The specialist realizes they packed an inbound delivery incorrectly on the first pass and needs to repack. The bot walks them through the recovery step by step: delete the existing handling units, use the Change Inbound Deliveries Fiori app to repack cleanly, then collect the new HU labels that print automatically from the assigned printer. This is the bot as a patient, step-by-step floor guide for a correction the specialist was never specifically trained on. And here is the important part — the bot knows the boundary of its own competence. If the new label does not come out of the Zebra printer automatically, and the bot was not designed or tested to troubleshoot printer output, it does not guess. It raises a ticket at the appropriate priority, assigns it to the right team, and tells the specialist it has done so. The specialist isn't left re-framing the same question to a hypercare consultant three times — the escalation happens cleanly and the resolution pace improves.

Production — the supervisor whose stock won't appear in MES. The production supervisor cannot see stock in their MES that should have synced from the EWM monitor. This is one of the most common and most painful go-live issues in a manufacturing landscape — an integration failure across the ERP–MES–EWM chain, where a posting in one system doesn't propagate to the next. The bot is trained to do exactly what a consultant would do to triage it: it takes a screenshot and/or the relevant data, optionally a line or two of context, and works through the integration checkpoints — did the material document post, did the EWM stock update, did the message reach MES — to isolate where the chain broke. If it can resolve it, it does. If it can't fully resolve it, the request routes to a real person already engaged on the project, with the triage already done.

The Bot Learns Only When a Trusted Human Signs Off

Those last two scenarios carry the most important design principle in the whole concept, so I want to state it plainly.

When a consultant resolves an escalated ticket, they cannot simply comment "issue fixed" and close it. That is how knowledge gets lost today. For the bot, a resolution only enters its knowledge base when a Solution Architect — or a peer of equivalent standing and trajectory — signs off that the resolution is correct, complete, and safe for the bot to repeat. Then the bot is retested against it, exactly as a configuration change would be retested.

This is the validation gate that separates this concept from a chatbot that confidently amplifies wrong answers. The bot relearns continuously — but only verified, signed-off knowledge, never an unreviewed "fixed" comment. A wrong resolution learned at machine speed and repeated to every shift is far more dangerous than no resolution at all. The human sign-off is not bureaucracy. It is the safety mechanism that makes self-learning trustworthy.

In all four scenarios: the error never had to be described in a second language, the consultant who built the config didn't have to be awake, the boundary of the bot's competence was respected, and every genuinely new resolution was captured — under human sign-off — for the next worker who hits it.

On the Architecture — Briefly

I'm keeping the stack high-level on purpose, because the originality here is when you build the agent and who curates it, not the technology. At a glance: a vision-capable LLM layer for the screenshot interpretation; a vector knowledge store trained on the project's own SIT/UAT defects, resolutions, and configuration documentation; integration into the implementation's ticketing system (the standard enterprise ITSM tool) so resolved incidents feed back through the SA validation gate; and a floor-friendly channel the workers already use. In an embedded EWM landscape it reads the same error and application-log surfaces the consultants already read — the difference is that it reads them at 5 AM, in every language, for every shift, and never forgets a resolution. The architecture is the easy part. The discipline of building and validating it alongside the warehouse processes is the hard part — and the valuable one.

Make It Non-Negotiable

Here's why this stays a concept unless the commercial model changes: as long as an AI hypercare agent is an optional add-on, it gets value-engineered out of the SOW on every budget-constrained project — which is all of them.

So don't make it optional. Price it into the implementation as a contracted hypercare deliverable, the same way you'd price the cutover plan or the training material. The economics are not subtle: the build cost of the agent is less than two weeks of a single floor consultant's hypercare time. One avoided week of go-live chaos pays for it. The argument to the client isn't "buy our AI" — it's "your hypercare will cost less and work better, and here's the line item."

That single reframing — from add-on to contracted deliverable — is what turns this from a nice idea into something that ships.

The Tool Does Not Yet Exist. That Is the Point.

I want to be clear and honest: I have not built this. What I have is five go-lives of watching the same preventable failure, a design that addresses each of its structural causes, and the conviction that the field is about to need it.

Every meaningful innovation in SAP consulting started as a named concept before it became a product. So I'm naming this one. An AI hypercare agent — built in parallel with testing, SA-curated, signed off through SIT and UAT like any warehouse process, socialized by OCM so the floor already trusts it, screenshot-first, and contracted as a deliverable — is, as far as I can find across SAP Community, SAP's roadmap, and the major SI tooling, not something anyone has yet put together this way.

I'd genuinely like to be challenged on that. If you've seen this built, tell me where — I want to learn from it. If you've lived the 6 AM frozen-screen scene too, tell me what you would add to the design. And if you're a Solution Architect or a delivery lead reading this before your next go-live: ask whether your hypercare plan is a stack of rotating consultants and hope, or something that actually holds the knowledge and stands in front of your floor as first-level support from hour one.

The scene I described plays out on warehouse floors every single week. We have the tools to end it. We've just been building them at the wrong time.

Yogesh Agarwal is an Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist with 15+ years across SAP EWM and TM implementations in food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, and industrial sectors. He writes about SAP supply chain execution on SAP Community and LinkedIn.

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Build Partner Products and Bring Them to SAP Store
entry.content:
Do you want to offer your ABAP Cloud solution to your customers as a cloud product?

SAP BTP ABAP Environment enables partners to build cloud products and side-by-side extensions with ABAP Cloud on SAP BTP and bring them to market via SAP Store. These products run on SAP BTP and can integrate with SAP S/4HANA, other SAP solutions and non-SAP solutions.

To support this journey, SAP BTP ABAP Environment Product Management offers a free-of-charge engagement for partners who want to build and commercialize products on SAP BTP ABAP Environment.

Who is this for?

This engagement is intended for SAP partners who:

  • want to offer a product to multiple customers
  • are planning a side-by-side solution on SAP BTP instead of an on-stack SAP S/4HANA extension
  • are considering SAP BTP ABAP Environment as their product platform
  • want to publish their solution via SAP Store

To commercialize and publish a partner product via SAP Store, an SAP PartnerEdge Build agreement is required. If you are not yet part of SAP PartnerEdge Build, we can discuss the required next steps in the initial conversation.

Supported delivery options

We currently support two delivery options for partner products on SAP BTP ABAP Environment:

Multitenant SaaS: The partner offers and operates the solution as a cloud service. Customers subscribe to the service and receive their own tenant in the partner's provider system.

Add-On Product: The partner offers the solution as software. Customers run their own SAP BTP ABAP Environment instance and consume the partner product in their own landscape, with the option to extend it according to their needs.

Both options enable partners to productize cloud solutions depending on their target customers, operating model and business model. Partners can also offer both delivery options in parallel, as most of the underlying steps are identical.

How we support you

As part of the engagement, SAP BTP ABAP Environment Product Management can support you with:

  • choosing the right delivery option
  • validating your product scenario and architecture
  • answering technical questions during implementation
  • sharing reference materials and tutorials
  • preparing your solution for SAP Store publication
  • moving towards go-live

The goal is to help partners bring their products live on SAP BTP ABAP Environment faster.

Interested?

If your company is planning to build a partner product on SAP BTP ABAP Environment, we would be happy to hear from you.

Please contact us: sap_btp_abap_environment@sap.com

To help us prepare the first discussion, please include a short description of your product idea or existing solution, your target customer segment, current development stage, and preferred delivery option if already known.

We will then set up a remote session to better understand your scenario and discuss how we can support your journey.

 

Helpful resources

To get started, you may find the following resources useful: 

  • Building partner products on SAP BTP ABAP Environment
  • Overview of delivery options: Multitenant SaaS and Add-On Product
  • Tutorial: MT SaaS Partner Reference Application
  • PE Build onboarding

entry.title: Bypassing the List Report in SAP Fiori Apps: BCMO, Static Keys, and Dynamic Keys
entry.content:
If you've ever generated a Business Configuration Maintenance Object (BCMO) app or built a standard List Report Fiori app for a table with just one relevant record, you've probably run into the same annoyance: the app insists on showing you a list page first, even when there's really only one record, or one record per user, that anyone should ever land on.

In this post, I'll walk through the complete flow: generating a BCMO app for a custom table, getting rid of the pointless singleton list report, and then bypassing the list page in a regular List Report app using both a static key and a dynamic key, such as the logged-in username.

What is a BCMO App, and How Does It Relate to Table Maintenance Generator (TMG)?

Before we jump into the how, a quick primer for anyone new to this.

A Table Maintenance Generator (TMG) is the classic SAP GUI way of generating a maintenance screen for a customizing table, using transaction SE11/SE54. You get a generated SM30 style screen where users can create, edit, and delete entries in a table.

BCMO, Business Configuration Maintenance Object, is essentially the RAP and Fiori based evolution of the same idea. Instead of generating an SM30 style dynpro, ADT generates a full RAP based application stack, behavior definition, behavior implementation, service definition, and service binding, for your custom table, and exposes it as a modern Fiori app. You get the same core purpose as TMG, maintaining configuration or master data in a table, but with a UI5 based List Report and Object Page instead of a classic table control screen.

So think of BCMO as: you want the SM30/TMG experience, but as a proper Fiori Elements app, generated automatically from your table.

You can access a BCMO app in two ways:

  • Through the Custom Business Configurations (CBC) app, where all the configuration and settings are automatically applied.
  • As a standalone app, published separately to your Fiori Launchpad or SAP Build Work Zone. In this case, the actual table maintenance appears on the object page, but you also get a singleton list report page in front of it, which is usually unnecessary.

That useless singleton list report is exactly what we're going to eliminate below.

Part 1: Creating a BCMO App

Prerequisites on the Database Table

Before ADT will let you generate a BCMO app, your custom table needs to satisfy a few conditions. If you skip these, generation will fail, and you'll typically see errors pointing you toward three things:

  • The table's delivery class needs to be set to C, since BCMO is meant for customizing tables, not regular application data.
  • Data maintenance needs to be explicitly allowed on the table, via the AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance annotation.
  • Character-like key fields need to be based on a domain, rather than a plain built-in type, so the generator can derive proper value help and type information.

Once these three conditions are met, ADT will happily generate the BCMO stack for you. Here's an example table definition that satisfies all of them:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product Industry Mapping'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zsac_t_prod_ind {

  key client          : abap.clnt not null;
  key industry_sector : mbrsh not null;
  unit_of_measure     : meins;
  currency_code       : abap.cuky;

}

Generating the BCMO Objects

Right-click on the table in ADT, click Generate ABAP Repository Objects, select Business Configuration Maintenance Object, and click Next.

Enter the package.

In the Configure Generator step, you decide the naming of the generated objects and which features to enable. For this example, we'll set Transport Selection to No Transport and uncheck "Enable Transport Selection Strip" to keep things simple. Depending on your landscape, you may want this enabled instead.

Here's the full list of objects that get generated for us:

After selecting your transport request, or choosing No Transport, click Finish, and sip a cup of tea while ADT does its thing.

Once generation completes, this is what you get in ADT:

Accessing the BCMO App

You can access your BCMO app in two ways:

  1. Through the Custom Business Configurations (CBC) app, where all the standard settings are already applied for you.
  2. As a standalone app, published separately to your SAP Fiori Launchpad or SAP Build Work Zone.

If you go the standalone route, the actual table maintenance will appear correctly on the object page, but you'll also get an unnecessary singleton list report page in front of it, which is exactly the problem we're solving in the next section.

Now open the service binding, publish it, and test it.

A Note on Authorizations

You may notice that an access control object gets automatically generated as part of BCMO. If the authorization object S_TABU_NAM is not assigned to your user role, you may see no data in the output, or the Edit button may be disabled.

In that case, you have two options:

  • Delete the access control on your root entity, or
  • Assign the required authorizations to your user ID.

While testing this, I also had to comment out the global authorization check in the generated behavior implementation just to see data, due to missing authorizations in my BTP trial account. Obviously, don't do this in a real system, it's purely a local testing workaround.

Part 2: Getting Rid of the Singleton List Report for a BCMO App

Now that our BCMO app is ready, let's get rid of the singleton list report. It doesn't add any value, there's only ever one relevant "list," so why make the user click through it every time?

Step 1: Create a New Project in BAS

Go to SAP Business Application Studio and create a new project from the available templates.

Select SAP Fiori Generator.

Select List Report Page and click Next.

Select the Data Source and Service.

Note: If your system isn't logged in yet, log in first. If you're on an SAP BTP trial account, you'll need to connect BAS to Cloud Foundry before you can see the available Service Bindings.

To do that, open the top search bar and type:

>CF: Login to Cloud Foundry

Log in with your credentials or SSO, then reopen the Data Source and Service Selection step.

Once logged in, you should now be able to see the service. Look for your Service Binding for the BCMO app and select it.

Select the entity.

If you're planning to deploy the app immediately, make sure to check "Add Deployment Configuration" and "Add SAP Fiori Launchpad Configuration." For this demo, I'm keeping both unchecked since we're only focused on getting rid of the list report page, not deployment.

Click Finish, and go have another cup of tea while the project scaffolds.

Step 2: Remove the List Report Route from the Manifest

With the project created in BAS, let's get to the main task.

Open manifest.json under the webapp folder and remove the list report route and its corresponding target.

We do this because the default routing configuration always points the app to the list report as the landing page first. By removing this route and target pair, we prevent the router from ever resolving to the list report on initial load, which sets us up to redirect straight to the object page instead.

Step 3: Auto-Navigate to the Object Page in Component.js

Now go to Component.js under the webapp folder and add the following code so the app routes directly to the object page of our singleton record whenever it loads:

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);
    this.getRouter().navTo("ProductIndSingletonObjectPage", { key: "(1)" }, true);
}

Step 4: Test It

Run the following command in the terminal to preview the app:

npm run start

And voilà, the first page that opens is no longer the singleton list report, but the actual object page we wanted users to land on directly.

From here, you can continue with deployment as per your requirement, including Fiori Launchpad configuration and transport.

Part 3: Bypassing the List Report for a Standard List Report App

The technique above works great for a true singleton, a table with essentially one record. But what if you're working with a regular List Report app on a table with multiple records, and you want to skip the list page in favor of a specific object page, based on a static key or a dynamic key like the logged-in user? Let's look at both.

Deploying the Base List Report App

As before, start by creating a new project in SAP BAS or VS Code, selecting SAP Fiori Application, then List Report as the template.

In the template wizard, select your Data Source, System, and Service, then click Next. Select the correct main entity and navigation entity, and choose Yes to automatically add table columns and a section to the object page.

Enter your project attribute details, choose Yes for deployment configuration, click Finish, and wait for the dependencies to install. Don't refresh the page while you see "Installing Dependencies" at the bottom right of VS Code.

Once deployed, remove the singleton list report the same way described above: strip the route and target from the manifest, and add the navTo call in Component.js.

The difference this time is what key you pass into that navTo call.

Option A: Bypassing with a Static Key

If you always want the app to open a specific, known record, hardcode the key directly:

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);
    this.getRouter().navTo("TableGroupAllObjectPage", { key: "(1)" }, true);
}

This is the simplest approach and works well when there's genuinely one record everyone should see, similar to the BCMO singleton pattern above, but applied manually to a regular List Report app.

Option B: Bypassing with a Dynamic Key (Logged-in Username)

Often, though, you don't want everyone landing on the same record. You want each user routed to their own record, based on who's logged in. For this, we fetch the current username first, then use it to construct the key dynamically before navigating.

init: function () {
    Component.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);

    var sUser = sap.ushell.Container
        ? sap.ushell.Container.getUser().getId()
        : "DEFAULT_USER";

    this.getRouter().navTo("TableGroupAllObjectPage", { key: "('" + sUser + "')" }, true);
}

A few things to keep in mind with the dynamic key approach:

  • Make sure the key field in your entity actually corresponds to something like a username, or a field you can reliably derive from the logged-in user, for example, mapped via a custom table or CDS view.
  • If no record exists for that user yet, decide upfront how your app should behave: show an empty object page in create mode, show a "no data" message, or fall back to a default record.
  • Test this thoroughly with multiple user IDs before deploying, since the whole point is that different users see different landing records.

For Multi-Level Apps

If your app has more than two navigation levels, for example, a list report page, then an object page showing a related sub-entity's list, then a further object page for that sub-entity's own details, you'll need to add another navigation node to the page map.

To do this, right-click on the app's folder in BAS and select Show Page Map. You'll see the existing levels, with a plus icon enabled on the deepest Object Page. Click it, select the correct navigation property, and add the new page.

Once your routing is set up correctly, deploy using:

npm run deploy

Wrapping Up

Whether you're working with a BCMO app generated straight from ADT, or a hand-built List Report Fiori app, the pattern for bypassing the list page is fundamentally the same:

  1. Remove the list report's route and target from the manifest.
  2. Add a navTo call in Component.js's init function that routes straight to the object page.
  3. Decide whether that route needs a static key, same record for everyone, or a dynamic key, per-user record, typically derived from the logged-in username.
  4. If you have deeper navigation levels, extend the page map accordingly before deploying.

This small change removes a genuinely pointless click for your end users, and it's one of those RAP and Fiori details that separates a generated-and-shipped app from a genuinely polished one.

If deployment configuration for RAP apps to SAP BTP is something you'd like a refresher on before trying this end to end, it's worth reading up on that step separately once you're ready to move beyond local testing.

entry.title: Chat-based RAP Business Object Generation in VS Code
entry.content:
With ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for VS Code, you can now generate complete RAP Business Objects directly from chat prompts. Describe your business scenario in natural language, and GitHub Copilot generates the corresponding RAP artifacts in your ABAP system.

The generated artifacts include:

  • Database tables
  • CDS views
  • Behavior definitions
  • Service definitions
  • Service bindings

This feature is enabled by the extension’s built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, which exposes ABAP backend APIs as tools that AI agents can invoke directly. This lets them create and connect the necessary RAP artifacts on your behalf.

 

Getting Started

For more information about setting up ADT for VS Code, configuring GitHub Copilot Agent Mode, using the ADT MCP server, and troubleshooting common issues, refer to the guide.

Prerequisites:

Before you begin, ensure that you have the following:

  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code installed from the VS Code Marketplace
  • An ABAP system connection configured in the extension (steps shown below)

Figure 1. Adding an ABAP system to the workspaceFigure 2. Selecting a destinationFigure 3. Log on with your credentials

 

  • GitHub Copilot with Agent Mode enabled

Figure 4. GitHub Copilot in agent mode

 

Enable the ADT MCP Server

First, verify that the ADT MCP server is enabled. To do so:

  1. Open the Settings and Search for ADT MCP Server in the search bar
  2. Check the box Enable ADT MCP Server

Figure 5. Enabling ADT MCP Server in settings

 

Configuring ADT MCP Server

The ADT MCP server is not automatically configured for GitHub Copilot. To configure the ADT MCP Server, open the Command Palette and run MCP: List Servers, then start the ADT MCP server.

Figure 6. Listing the available MCP servers

Figure 7. Starting the ADT MCP Server (part 1)Figure 8. Starting the ADT MCP Server (part 2)

 

When the ADT MCP server is running and successfully connected, the following notification appears in the lower-right corner:

Figure 9. Success message

 

Once connected, the available ABAP MCP tools appear in GitHub Copilot. You can verify this by opening Configure Tools in the GitHub Copilot chat, where you can view and select the tools that are active for your session.

Figure 10. Verifying the connection to the ABAP MCP Server (part 1)Figure 11. Verifying the connection to the ADT MCP Server (part 2)

 

If MCP tools don't appear after setup please refer to troubleshooting

 

Generating a RAP Business Object

Note: Before running the generation, ensure that your target package already exists. Currently, new packages must be created in ADT for Eclipse. Creating packages directly from ADT for VS Code will be supported in a future release.

With the ADT MCP server running, open GitHub Copilot Chat in Agent Mode and describe the RAP Business Object you want to generate. For best results you can take a look at agent prompt best practices.

An example prompt would be:

“ Generate a RAP Business Object for conference and tech event management, including a database table, CDS views,

 behavior definition, and an OData V4 UI service.

  • Use the Package TEST_BB_CONFERENCE_MGT
  • Use the following specification:
    • Entity 1: Event (root entity)
    • Entity 2: Session, child of Event
    • Entity 3: Speaker, child of Event
    • Entity 4: Registration, child of Session“

Figure 12. Entering a prompt in the chatFigure 13. GitHub Copilot response to the prompt

After your prompt, Copilot will:

  • List available destinations (abap_list_destinations)
  • Retrieve the schema (abap_generators-get_schema)
  • Validate (abap_creation-run_validation)
  • Create a transport request (abap_transport-create)
  • Generate all objects (abap_generators-generate_objects)
  • Activate objects (abap_activate_objects)

After the generation completes, refresh your ABAP project by opening the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) and running ABAP: Refresh. The generated RAP artifacts will then appear in your workspace under the target package.

Figure 14. RAP artifacts displayed under the defined package

 

Once the service binding appears in your workspace, use the Publish CodeLens action to publish the service directly from VS Code. After publishing, the OData service is available for consumption.

Figure 15. Publishing the service bindingFigure 16. Success message

 

Then, use the Preview CodeLens to launch a live preview of your Fiori elements application directly in your browser:

Figure 17. Launching the application previewFigure 18. Selecting the entity for preview

 

Your generated application is then displayed in the browser:

Figure 19. Application preview

 

All generated artifacts are created directly in your ABAP system as standard repository objects. They are identical to the RAP artifacts you would create manually, fully editable in ADT for VS Code, and can be extended using the standard RAP development workflow.

 

Summary

Chat-based RAP Business Object generation enables you to create complete RAP Business Objects directly from natural language prompts in ADT for VS Code. Combined with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode and the ADT MCP server, it automates the creation of the required RAP artifacts while keeping them fully editable as standard ABAP repository objects.

For more information, including setup instructions and configuration details, see the ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Join Us Tomorrow: SAP BTP ABAP Environment Customer & Partner Roundtable – July 2026
entry.content:
Dear customers and partners, 

Join us tomorrow for the next session of our SAP BTP ABAP Environment Customer & Partner Roundtable, taking place on July 07, 2026, from 10:00–11:00 CET.

Our roundtable is a monthly virtual meeting where we share the latest updates from the product team and showcase selected topics from our experts. Join the session

SAP BTP ABAP Environment Team
